quick_facts.label: Core function
quick_facts.value: Delivers competency-based, trade-specific vocational instruction, combining practical skill transfer with training methodology so learners can meet defined vocational outcomes.

quick_facts.label: Key preparation route
quick_facts.value: CITS is DGT’s principal instructor-training route. DGT states that eligible entrants hold an NTC, NAC, diploma or degree; successful CITS trainees receive the National Craft Instructor Certificate (NCIC). Trade-specific eligibility must be checked in the current admission material.

quick_facts.label: Private-ITI context
quick_facts.value: Private ITIs can operate within the DGT/NCVET affiliation ecosystem. DGT’s affiliation page publishes admission and affiliation actions for both government and private ITIs.

quick_facts.label: Standards orientation
quick_facts.value: Training is expected to align with NSQF-approved qualifications and defined learning outcomes; NSQF is an outcome- and competency-based framework covering vocational education, training, skilling and relevant experience.

quick_facts.label: Experience-based recognition route
quick_facts.value: For eligible in-service ITI instructors without NCIC, DGT’s CITS Recognition of Prior Learning route sets conditions including a relevant CTS/diploma/degree base qualification and at least three years of teaching experience; private-ITI candidates must meet the documentation and sponsorship/evidence requirements in the guideline.

sections.heading: What the role involves
paragraphs: A vocational instructor translates a trade curriculum into competency-based teaching: preparing training content for the relevant job role, delivering theory and hands-on practice, using suitable training and assessment methods, and supporting learners’ industry and job-market readiness. In a private institute, the precise timetable, trade portfolio, learner mix and administrative duties are set by the employer, but the instructional work should remain tied to the qualification outcomes being delivered.

CITS materials describe the instructor purpose as transferring hands-on skills and training skilled manpower for industry. NCVET also identifies training implementation, subject-matter preparation, current-practice application, learning strategies, assessment modes, industry connections and overall training-process management as trainer responsibilities.
bullets: Plan sessions around the approved occupational/qualification outcomes and practical tasks.
Demonstrate trade procedures, supervise workshop or laboratory practice, and give corrective feedback.
Use assessments and training records consistent with the institute’s programme and applicable qualification requirements.
Build employability awareness through industry-relevant practice, communication and workplace-readiness support.

sections.heading: Entry routes and credentials
paragraphs: A common route is first to build trade competence through an ITI/CTS qualification (NTC or NAC), a relevant diploma, or a relevant degree, then undertake CITS in the appropriate trade. DGT describes CITS as combining technical skill with training methodology and lists NTC, NAC, diploma and degree holders as eligible categories; exact prerequisites differ by CITS trade and admission cycle.

A B.Voc or another relevant degree can be a useful higher-education foundation where it matches the technical trade and the institute’s hiring requirements, but it is not a universal substitute for trade-specific eligibility. Candidates should compare their qualification against the current CITS trade entry criteria and the private institute’s vacancy notice.

An experienced instructor may instead be eligible for DGT’s CITS RPL process. The published guideline requires, among other conditions, a relevant CTS/diploma/degree qualification and at least three years of teaching experience; it also specifies evidence requirements for experience claimed at private ITIs.
bullets: Develop demonstrable competence in the trade you intend to teach.
Add formal vocational pedagogy through CITS or another applicable NCVET-recognized Training of Trainers route.
For each vacancy, verify the institute’s affiliation status, the trade being taught, required experience, and whether NCIC/CITS is specified.
Treat entrance tests, intake dates, course availability and certificate-validity rules as cycle-specific; confirm them from current DGT/NSTI notices before applying.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: The role needs both domain skills and platform skills. NCVET defines domain skills as sector-, subsector- or job-role-specific capability, and platform skills as transferable training skills, including vocational pedagogy. Its trainer guidance also points to learner-needs analysis, content development and delivery, appropriate methodology, communication, assessment, blended learning and impact assessment.

For trade instruction, safety-conscious practical demonstration, observation of performance, clear feedback and the ability to adapt explanations to learners with different prior preparation are especially important. These capabilities support the competency-based, outcome-focused design of NSQF-aligned training.
bullets: Current technical competence in the assigned trade and familiarity with industry practice.
Vocational pedagogy: lesson design, demonstrations, practice sequencing and learner support.
Assessment literacy: interpreting outcomes, observing performance and applying the required assessment strategy.
Communication, group facilitation, inclusivity and workplace-readiness coaching.
Digital and blended-training capability where the programme or employer uses it.

sections.heading: Where and how the work is done
paragraphs: The most direct setting is a private ITI or other private vocational-training provider delivering a defined trade. DGT’s current affiliation material explicitly covers NCVET-affiliated government and private ITIs, while its CITS guidance describes the national ITI and skill ecosystem as a major destination for trained instructors.

The work commonly spans classroom instruction and supervised practical learning in workshops, laboratories or simulated work areas. Where an ITI participates in DGT’s Dual System of Training, instructors may also coordinate learning that combines ITI-based theory with industry-based practical exposure; DGT says eligible government and private affiliated ITIs can run the scheme in their relevant affiliated trades.
bullets: Private ITIs affiliated within the national vocational-training system.
Private skill-development providers delivering short- or long-term programmes.
Industry-linked training arrangements, including ITI–industry dual-training models where applicable.
Corporate or industry training roles may be a related transition for instructors who can train workers and coordinate technical learning.

sections.heading: Progression options
paragraphs: With stronger instructional experience and recognised trainer preparation, a private vocational instructor can broaden into senior instruction, master-trainer work, curriculum or written instructional-material development, training coordination, corporate technical training, or assessment-related work. DGT’s CITS prospectus identifies instructor work in ITIs, certified delivery under skill-development schemes, industry training coordination and preparation of Written Instructional Materials as outcomes of CITS; NCVET separately describes Trainer and Master Trainer pathways through recognised Training of Trainers and Master Trainer programmes.

Assessment is a distinct progression direction rather than an automatic extension of teaching. NCVET publishes separate Training of Assessors guidelines, so a candidate seeking assessor work should check the current qualification, awarding-body and assessment-agency requirements.
bullets: Senior vocational instructor or lead trainer for a trade area.
Master Trainer after suitable recognised preparation and demonstrated training capability.
Corporate technical trainer or industry training coordinator.
Curriculum, learning-material or instructional-design work.
Skill assessor, subject to the applicable assessor-training and engagement requirements.
Vocational training centre entrepreneurship, subject to affiliation, infrastructure, staffing and local regulatory requirements.
